section.title: David's Charge to Solomon
section.sectionRationale: David assembles every leader of Israel in Jerusalem for a final, public charge. He explains why he, a man of war, was not permitted to build God's house, announces God's choice of Solomon, urges him to know God and serve Him with a whole heart, and hands over the temple's complete architectural plan, which he says came to him in writing from the LORD's own hand.
section.thesis: In his last great public act, David gathers all Israel's leadership to explain God's reversal of his own temple ambitions, to charge Solomon before the whole assembly to know the God of his father and serve Him with a whole heart and willing mind, and to hand over a temple pattern he insists came from the LORD Himself in writing, so that Solomon's obedience, and Israel's, would rest not on human invention but on a divinely given design and a divinely searched heart.
section.frameNote: Three movements: David explains before the assembly why God chose Solomon rather than him to build the house; his public charge to Solomon to know God and serve Him wholeheartedly; and the handing over of the temple's divinely given pattern, sealed with a final word of encouragement.

units.label: Why Solomon, not David, would build the house
units.note: a man of war, and God's chosen son

verses.text: Now David summoned all the leaders of Israel to Jerusalem: the leaders of the tribes, the leaders of the divisions in the king's service, the commanders of thousands and of hundreds, and the officials in charge of all the property and cattle of the king and his sons, along with the court officials and mighty men, every mighty man of valor.

Then King David rose to his feet and said, "Listen to me, my brothers and my people. It was in my heart to build a house as a resting place for the ark of the covenant of the LORD and as a footstool for our God. I had made preparations to build it,
but God said to me, 'You are not to build a house for My Name, because you are a man of war who has shed blood.'

Yet the LORD, the God of Israel, chose me out of all my father's house to be king over Israel forever. For He chose Judah as leader, and from the house of Judah He chose my father's household, and from my father's sons He was pleased to make me king over all Israel.
And of all my sons, for the LORD has given me many sons, He has chosen Solomon my son to sit on the throne of the kingdom of the LORD over Israel.
And He said to me, 'Solomon your son is the one who will build My house and My courts, for I have chosen him as My son, and I will be his Father.
I will establish his kingdom forever, if he resolutely carries out My commandments and ordinances, as is being done this day.'

units.label: The charge: know God, serve with a whole heart
units.note: before all Israel, the assembly of the LORD

verses.text: So now in the sight of all Israel, the assembly of the LORD, and in the hearing of our God, keep and seek out all the commandments of the LORD your God, so that you may possess this good land and leave it as an inheritance to your descendants forever.

As for you, Solomon my son, know the God of your father and serve Him {{levShalem|wholeheartedly}} and with a willing mind, for the LORD searches every heart and understands the intent of every thought. If you seek Him, He will be found by you; but if you forsake Him, He will reject you forever.
Consider now that the LORD has chosen you to build a house for the sanctuary. Be strong and do it."

units.label: The pattern from the LORD's own hand

verses.text: Then David gave his son Solomon the {{tabnit|plans}} for the portico of the temple, its buildings, storehouses, upper rooms, inner rooms, and the room for the mercy seat.
The plans contained everything David had in mind for the courts of the house of the LORD, for all the surrounding rooms, for the treasuries of the house of God and of the dedicated things,
for the divisions of the priests and Levites, for all the work of service in the house of the LORD, and for all the articles of service in the house of the LORD:
the weight of all the gold articles for every kind of service; the weight of all the silver articles for every kind of service;
the weight of the gold lampstands and their lamps, including the weight of each lampstand and its lamps; the weight of each silver lampstand and its lamps, according to the use of each lampstand;
the weight of gold for each table of showbread, and of silver for the silver tables;
the weight of the pure gold for the forks, sprinkling bowls, and pitchers; the weight of each gold dish; the weight of each silver bowl;
the weight of the refined gold for the altar of incense; and the plans for the chariot of the gold cherubim that spread their wings and overshadowed the ark of the covenant of the LORD.
"All this," said David, "all the details of this plan, the LORD has made clear to me in writing by His hand upon me."

David also said to Solomon his son, "Be strong and courageous, and do it. Do not be afraid or discouraged, for the LORD God, my God, is with you. He will neither fail you nor forsake you before all the work for the service of the house of the LORD is finished.

The divisions of the priests and Levites are ready for all the service of the house of God, and every willing man of every skill will be at your disposal for the work. The officials and all the people are fully at your command."

tabnit.gloss: Pattern, plan, model, likeness. David hands Solomon the tabnit of the temple, verse 11, and insists it came to him in writing from the LORD's own hand, verse 19, the same word used for the pattern of the tabernacle shown to Moses on the mountain, Exodus 25:9, 40, tying Solomon's temple to a design of divine, not merely human, origin.

levShalem.gloss: A whole, complete, undivided heart. David charges Solomon to serve God 'with a whole heart,' verse 9, the same standard David later prays God would give Solomon again, 29:19, naming the single most important qualification for building and worshiping rightly in God's house, wholehearted devotion rather than divided or merely outward obedience.

basic.context: In one of the last great public acts of his reign, David gathers every leader of Israel, tribal chiefs, military commanders, royal officials, and mighty men, to Jerusalem. Standing before them, he explains something Israel needed to hear plainly: it had been his own heart's desire to build God's house, but God had told him no, because David was a man of war who had shed blood. Instead, God chose Solomon, David's son, to build the temple and to sit on the LORD's own throne over Israel, with a conditional promise that his kingdom would be established forever if he kept God's commandments. David then turns directly to Solomon and charges him, in front of the entire assembly, to know the God of his father and serve Him with a whole heart and a willing mind, warning that the LORD searches every heart and that forsaking Him brings rejection. Finally, David hands Solomon an extraordinarily detailed architectural plan for the temple, its rooms, courts, treasuries, and every furnishing down to the weight of gold and silver required, insisting that the entire pattern had come to him in writing directly from the LORD's hand.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording a specific public transfer of both kingship and building responsibility from David to Solomon. It is not a manual for every believer to receive architectural blueprints from God, but its call to know God and serve Him with a whole heart is a standing, permanent charge for every reader. The claim that the temple's pattern came directly from the LORD is presented as a unique, extraordinary act of revelation, comparable to Moses receiving the tabernacle's pattern on Sinai, not a normal or repeatable experience.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter's most striking word is tabnit, pattern, the same term used of the tabernacle's heavenly pattern shown to Moses, Exodus 25:9, now applied to Solomon's temple and said to have come to David 'in writing from the hand of the LORD,' verse 19, an extraordinary claim of divine authorship behind Israel's greatest building project. And running through David's charge is the demand for a lev shalem, a whole heart, verse 9, the one quality David insists matters more than skill, wealth, or even the temple's grandeur itself.
basic.typology: The tabnit given to David, like the pattern shown to Moses for the tabernacle, points to the truth that true worship is never a human invention but must be received from God; the writer to the Hebrews explicitly reads the earthly sanctuary as a copy of heavenly realities, Hebrews 8:5, a trajectory this chapter's temple pattern extends. And the whole heart David demands of Solomon anticipates the new covenant promise of a heart no longer divided but truly transformed to obey God, Ezekiel 36:26 and Jeremiah 31:33.
basic.christ: David's charge to Solomon to know God and serve Him with a whole heart and willing mind, verse 9, states in miniature the very obedience every human king, including Solomon himself, would eventually fail to sustain, yet it is fulfilled perfectly in Jesus, the Son of David who always did what pleased the Father, John 8:29. And the tabnit, the divinely given pattern for the temple, anticipates the truth that the earthly sanctuary was always a copy and shadow of heavenly things, Hebrews 8:5, fulfilled when Christ Himself, and the church built on Him, become the true temple where God dwells, John 2:19-21 and Ephesians 2:19-22.
basic.god: God is the one who searches every heart and understands the intent of every thought, verse 9, holding out both the gracious invitation, 'if you seek Him, He will be found by you,' and the sober warning, 'if you forsake Him, He will reject you forever.' God is also the one who reveals, not merely permits, true worship, giving David the temple's entire pattern directly, so that Solomon's obedience would rest on divine revelation rather than human architectural genius. And God is faithful to His promises even through a flawed, war-marked king like David, redirecting rather than rejecting his desire to build, and assuring Solomon repeatedly, 'He will neither fail you nor forsake you.'
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 25:9, 40 records God showing Moses the pattern, tabnit, for the tabernacle on the mountain, the precedent for David's claim here. 2 Samuel 7:12-13 records God's earlier promise that David's son would build the house and have his throne established forever. 1 Kings 2:1-4 shows David repeating a similar charge to Solomon privately before his death. Hebrews 8:5 explains that the earthly sanctuary served as a copy and shadow of heavenly things, fulfilled in Christ.
basic.oneStory: David's public charge marks the handoff of both the throne and the temple's design from father to son, insisting that neither Israel's king nor Israel's worship rests on human invention: both come by God's own choice and revelation. The whole heart David demands of Solomon, and the divine pattern he hands over, together point forward to Christ, the greater Son of David who always sought His Father wholeheartedly and who is Himself the true temple where heaven and earth finally meet.

context.literary: This chapter forms the emotional and theological hinge between the long administrative lists of chapters 23 to 27 and David's climactic prayer and death in chapter 29. It is structured as a single public assembly with two audiences addressed in turn, first all Israel's leaders, verses 2-8, then Solomon directly, verses 9-10 and 20-21, framing the transfer of both throne and temple around a personal, wholehearted charge rather than mere administrative transition.
context.historical: Ancient Near Eastern building projects, especially royal temples, were typically credited to the king's own wisdom and resources; David's insistence that the pattern came 'in writing by His hand upon me,' verse 19, is a striking claim setting Israel's temple apart from every surrounding nation's man-made shrines. For the exiles rebuilding worship generations later, knowing that the first temple's very design was divinely revealed, not merely royally commissioned, gave weight and legitimacy to the pattern they sought to honor in their own restored worship.
context.canonical: This chapter deliberately echoes Moses receiving the tabernacle's pattern on Sinai, Exodus 25:9, placing Solomon's temple within the same trajectory of divinely revealed worship rather than human religious innovation. It also anticipates 1 Kings 2:1-4, where David repeats a similar charge privately, and ultimately the New Testament's teaching that the earthly sanctuary was only ever a shadow of heavenly, and finally Christ-centered, realities, Hebrews 8:5, 9:23-24.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record David's public explanation of why Solomon, not David himself, would build God's house, his direct charge to Solomon to know God and serve Him wholeheartedly, and his handing over of a temple pattern he insists was divinely revealed, so that Solomon's obedience and Israel's confidence in the coming temple would rest on God's own choice and revelation.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical transfer of throne and temple-building responsibility, including an extraordinary claim of direct revelation unique to this moment; it does not prescribe that believers today should expect architectural plans delivered by direct revelation. What is abidingly prescriptive is the charge itself, to know God and serve Him with a whole heart and willing mind, since the LORD searches every heart, a permanent standard for every reader.

debates.question: In what sense did the temple's pattern come to David 'in writing from the hand of the LORD,' verse 19?

views.label: Direct, verbal or written revelation, akin to Moses on Sinai
views.case: The language deliberately echoes Exodus 25:9 and 40, where God showed Moses the tabernacle's pattern; David's claim suggests a comparably direct, extraordinary act of revelation, perhaps through a prophet or vision, that he then had recorded in writing, making the temple's design as authoritative and non-negotiable as the tabernacle's had been.

views.label: Revelation mediated through the Spirit's inspiration on David's understanding
views.case: The BSB footnote notes the phrase can also be rendered 'had with him by the Spirit,' suggesting the pattern may have come through the Spirit's illumination of David's own careful planning and accumulated wisdom over years of preparation, rather than a single dramatic vision, though still genuinely from God rather than human invention alone.

debates.bearing: Either way, the text insists the temple's design was not David's or Solomon's personal creativity but a pattern given by God, grounding Israel's worship in divine revelation rather than human religious preference.

debates.question: Why does God's promise to establish Solomon's kingdom forever, verse 7, include the condition 'if he resolutely carries out My commandments'?

views.label: A real, historical conditionality for Solomon's personal reign
views.case: Unlike the unconditional, ultimate promise of an eternal Davidic throne in 2 Samuel 7:16, this verse's condition addresses Solomon's own immediate reign and its blessing; Solomon's later apostasy, 1 Kings 11:1-13, and the kingdom's eventual division show this conditional word was genuinely at stake and, in a real sense, not fully kept by Solomon himself.

views.label: A pastoral emphasis on obedience within an already secure covenant
views.case: The unconditional Davidic covenant, 2 Samuel 7, guarantees an eternal throne regardless of any single king's failure, ultimately secured in Christ; this verse's conditional language functions primarily as pastoral exhortation urging Solomon toward faithfulness and blessing within his own reign, not a threat to the covenant's ultimate, unconditional fulfillment.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the ultimate, unconditional promise of an everlasting Davidic throne, fulfilled in Christ, stands secure, while individual kings, including Solomon, remained genuinely responsible and answerable for their own obedience within that larger covenant framework.

study.originalLanguages: The heart of this chapter is the tabnit, the pattern, the same word used of the tabernacle's design shown to Moses on the mountain, Exodus 25:9, now applied to the temple and said to have reached David in writing from the LORD's own hand, verse 19, an extraordinary claim tying Solomon's building project directly to divine revelation rather than royal invention. And David's charge to Solomon centers on the lev shalem, the whole heart, verse 9, the LORD searching every heart and understanding every intent, so that knowledge of God and wholehearted service, not merely outward performance, mark the one qualification that matters most for building and inhabiting God's house rightly.

typology.type: The tabnit, the divinely revealed pattern for the temple
typology.fulfillment: The earthly sanctuary as a copy and shadow of heavenly realities, fulfilled and surpassed in Christ, the true temple, Hebrews 8:5 and John 2:19-21.

typology.type: The whole heart David demands of Solomon
typology.fulfillment: The new covenant promise of a transformed, undivided heart, given fully in Christ and by His Spirit, Ezekiel 36:26 and Jeremiah 31:33.

typology.type: The conditional charge, 'if he resolutely carries out My commandments'
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the greater Son of David, who perfectly and unfailingly kept His Father's commandments, securing forever what Solomon and every human king could not, John 8:29 and Hebrews 4:15.

study.christ: David's charge to Solomon, to know the God of his father and serve Him wholeheartedly and with a willing mind, verse 9, describes exactly the obedience Solomon himself would eventually fail to sustain, 1 Kings 11:1-13, yet it is fulfilled perfectly in Jesus, the true Son of David, who could say, 'I always do what pleases Him,' John 8:29. The tabnit, the pattern David insists came directly from the LORD's hand, anticipates the New Testament's teaching that Israel's whole sanctuary system was a copy and shadow of heavenly things, Hebrews 8:5, now realized in Christ, who is Himself the temple where God dwells among His people, John 2:19-21, and in the church, being built together into a dwelling place for God by the Spirit, Ephesians 2:19-22.
study.god: God is the one who searches every heart and understands the intent of every thought, verse 9, offering both a gracious promise, 'if you seek Him, He will be found by you,' and a sober warning, 'if you forsake Him, He will reject you forever.' He is also the God who reveals true worship rather than leaving it to human invention, entrusting David with the temple's entire pattern so that Solomon's obedience would rest on divine design, not architectural genius. And He is unfailingly faithful, redirecting rather than discarding David's own desire to build, and promising Solomon repeatedly across this chapter that He will neither fail him nor forsake him until the work is finished.

crossReferences.note: God shows Moses the tabnit, the pattern, for the tabernacle on the mountain, the precedent behind David's claim of a divinely given temple design.
God's earlier promise that David's son would build the house and have his throne established forever, the covenant background to this chapter's charge.
David repeats a similar charge to Solomon privately shortly before his death, reinforcing the call to wholehearted obedience.
Solomon's later failure to keep a whole heart toward the LORD, showing the seriousness of the charge given here in verse 9.
The earthly sanctuary served only as a copy and shadow of heavenly things, fulfilled in Christ, the reality behind the temple's divine pattern.

study.oneStory: David's public charge hands both the throne and the temple's design from father to son, insisting that neither Israel's king nor Israel's worship was ever a human invention: both rest on God's own choice and God's own revealed pattern. The whole heart David demanded of Solomon, a standard Solomon himself would not fully keep, and the divinely given tabnit for the temple both point forward to Christ, the greater Son of David who always sought His Father wholeheartedly and who is Himself the true and final temple where God dwells with His people.

quiz.q: Why did God tell David he could not build the temple, according to verse 3?

options.text: Because David was a man of war who had shed blood.
options.note: Verse 3 gives this exact reason in God's own words to David.

options.text: Because David had personally sinned by conducting a census of Israel.
options.note: The census, recalled in chapters 21 and 27, was a real and separate failure, but the specific reason given here for not building the temple is David's life as a man of war and bloodshed.

options.text: Because David lacked the wealth needed to build such a grand temple.
options.note: David's vast provision of gold, silver, and materials, detailed especially in chapter 29, shows wealth was never the obstacle.

options.text: Because David had not yet secured Israel from all its surrounding enemies.
options.note: David's many wars are indeed the reason his hands were marked by bloodshed, but the text's stated reason is specifically that he was a man of war who had shed blood, not merely that peace had not yet arrived.

quiz.q: What did David charge Solomon to do above all else, according to verse 9?

options.text: To know the God of his father and serve Him wholeheartedly and with a willing mind.
options.note: Verse 9 states this charge directly, the central demand of the whole chapter.

options.text: To finish building the temple as quickly as possible.
options.note: Building the temple is the practical task at hand, but the deeper charge concerns Solomon's heart and knowledge of God, not mere speed of construction.

options.text: To surpass his father David in wealth and military conquest.
options.note: The charge is entirely about wholehearted devotion to God, not personal ambition for wealth or conquest.

options.text: To follow all of David's specific instructions without deviation.
options.note: Obedience to the given pattern and instructions matters greatly in this chapter, but the deepest charge in verse 9 is to know and serve God Himself wholeheartedly, the root from which faithful obedience would flow.

quiz.q: According to David, where did the temple's detailed architectural plan come from?

options.text: The LORD made it clear to David in writing by His hand upon him.
options.note: Verse 19 records David's own explicit claim about the plan's divine origin.

options.text: David designed it himself, drawing on his experience building his own palace.
options.note: David explicitly credits the LORD, not his own design experience, as the source of the plan.

options.text: Through the Spirit's illumination of David's own careful, lifelong preparations for the temple.
options.note: The BSB footnote on verse 12 notes this phrase can be rendered 'had with him by the Spirit,' a real and true reading, but David's own explicit words in verse 19 stress the plan came in writing from the LORD's hand, more direct than illumined planning alone.

options.text: Foreign architects familiar with other nations' temples supplied the design.
options.note: No foreign source is mentioned; David explicitly and repeatedly credits the LORD as the plan's origin.

quiz.q: What warning does David give Solomon alongside the promise that God can be found?

options.text: That if Solomon forsakes God, He will reject him forever.
options.note: Verse 9 pairs the promise 'if you seek Him, He will be found by you' with this sober warning about forsaking God.

options.text: That Solomon would lose his throne to a foreign nation immediately.
options.note: The warning given is about God's rejection for forsaking Him, not an immediate prediction of foreign conquest.

options.text: That forsaking God would bring serious spiritual consequences for Solomon.
options.note: True as a general summary, but it softens the specific, severe wording David actually uses, that God would reject him forever, verse 9.

options.text: That Solomon's brothers would rise up against him if he failed.
options.note: No such warning about his brothers appears in this charge; the warning is specifically about Solomon's own standing before God.

quiz.q: What assurance did David give Solomon at the close of his charge, verse 20?

options.text: That the LORD God would be with him and would neither fail him nor forsake him until the temple's work was finished.
options.note: Verse 20 gives this direct assurance, echoing language used of Joshua's commissioning, Joshua 1:5, 9.

options.text: That priests, Levites, and every willing skilled worker would be fully at his disposal for the work.
options.note: True and stated in verse 21, but this is the assurance of human help that follows; the specific assurance in verse 20 itself is God's own abiding presence and faithfulness.

options.text: That Solomon alone, without any help, must complete every detail personally.
options.note: Verse 21 explicitly promises that priests, Levites, and every willing skilled worker would be at Solomon's disposal for the work.

options.text: That Solomon would face no opposition at all in building the temple.
options.note: The assurance given is God's abiding presence and faithfulness, not a promise of a trouble-free building process.

questions: David's heartfelt desire to build God's house was redirected, not rejected outright, and given to his son instead. When has God redirected a good desire of yours rather than simply refusing it, and how did you respond?

David charged Solomon to know God and serve Him with a whole heart, not merely to perform religious duties correctly. Where in your life might your service to God have become more about outward performance than a genuinely whole, willing heart?

David insisted the temple's pattern came from God, not his own imagination, even though he had clearly thought deeply about every detail. How do you discern the difference between your own good ideas and what God is actually calling you to do?

David repeatedly assured Solomon, 'He will neither fail you nor forsake you,' language echoing God's promise to Joshua. What large, intimidating task is God calling you to right now, and how does this same promise apply to you in Christ?

The chapter ends with the priests, Levites, and willing skilled workers all placed at Solomon's disposal for the work. Who are the people God has placed alongside you to help accomplish what He has called you to do, and are you receiving that help well?

translationNotes.note: The BSB renders a long list of officials as a single sentence describing everyone David summoned; a source em dash before the final phrase 'every mighty man of valor' was converted to a comma.

The BSB footnote notes that the Septuagint supports including 'of the temple' after 'portico,' while the Hebrew text as it stands omits this phrase; the sense of the temple's entrance hall is retained either way.

The BSB footnote notes that the phrase 'everything David had in mind' can also be rendered 'had with him by the Spirit,' leaving open whether the plan's transmission is described more as direct dictation or Spirit-given understanding.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 28:1-21. Source em dashes, including in verses 1 and 5, were converted to commas; no dashes remain in this file.
